claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 20, which is 20 or over

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and the child or children you're asking about.

Let me start with a few basic questions:

1. **Do you have a child or children you're responsible for?** If yes, how many children are you asking about?

2. **What are the ages of the child/children?**

Once I understand your basic situation, I'll ask some follow-up questions to properly assess your eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I have three children I'm asking about. 

My eldest, Alex, is 20 years old. Then there's Blake who is 16, and my youngest, Charlie, is 5.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have three children: Alex (20), Blake (16), and Charlie (5).

Let me ask some follow-up questions to assess eligibility for each of them:

**Starting with Alex (20 years old):**

Since Alex is 20, Child Benefit typically stops at age 16 (or up to age 20 if the young person is in approved education or training). 

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** For example:
- Full-time non-advanced education (like A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs

Please note that university or advanced education doesn't count for Child Benefit purposes.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. Actually, they've been in hospital for quite a while now - about three months or so. I still regularly buy things for them though, like clothes and their favorite snacks and magazines.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand Alex has been in hospital for about three months.

Let me ask a few more questions about Alex's situation:

1. **Is Alex in hospital full-time** (meaning they're staying there continuously), or are they coming home between treatments?

2. **Are the hospital costs being paid by the NHS**, or is this private healthcare?

3. You mentioned you buy things for Alex - do you also **contribute to the cost of Alex's accommodation or maintenance** while they're in hospital?

After we clarify Alex's situation, I'll also need to ask some questions about Blake and Charlie to complete the assessment for all three children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is in hospital full-time - they've been staying there continuously. It's NHS, not private. And yes, I do contribute - I regularly spend money on things Alex needs, like I mentioned, their clothes, magazines, and favorite foods and things like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

Now, regarding the hospital stay - there are specific rules about Child Benefit when a child is in hospital for an extended period:

**Has Alex been in hospital continuously for more than 12 weeks (84 days)?** You mentioned about three months - is it more or less than 12 weeks exactly?

This is important because if a child is in full-time hospital care paid for by the NHS for more than 12 weeks, it can affect Child Benefit eligibility, even if you're contributing to their costs.

After we establish this, I'll move on to ask about Blake and Charlie.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's been just over 12 weeks - it's been 13 weeks now actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 20 years old, not in approved education or training, and has been in full-time NHS hospital care for more than 12 weeks, this will affect eligibility for Alex specifically.

Now let me ask about **Blake (16 years old)**:

1. **Is Blake still in full-time education or training?** For example, studying for GCSEs, A-levels, or in an approved training program?

2. **Does Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake doesn't live with me - they live elsewhere. They've just recently left school and signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next. But they're also working full-time at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Blake.

Let me ask a few follow-up questions about Blake's situation:

1. **Where does Blake live?** Is it with another parent, a relative, or somewhere else?

2. **Do you contribute to Blake's upkeep?** For example, do you provide money for their living costs, buy them clothes, food, or other necessities on a regular basis?

3. You mentioned Blake is working full-time and registered with the careers service - **are they enrolled in any approved training program**, or just registered with the careers service while working?

After this, I'll ask about Charlie to complete the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with their other parent. I don't contribute anything financially at the moment - I'm not able to help out with money right now. And no, Blake isn't enrolled in any training program - they're just registered with the careers service while working full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying Blake's situation.

Now let me ask about **Charlie (5 years old)**:

1. **Does Charlie live with you?**

2. **Are you responsible for Charlie** - meaning do you provide for their day-to-day care and financial needs?

3. **Is Charlie in any special circumstances** - for example, in hospital, in local authority care, or living with someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives with me at home. I'm responsible for them - I provide their day-to-day care and all their needs. And no, there are no special circumstances - they're just living at home with me like a normal 5-year-old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask a couple of final questions to complete the assessment:

1. **Are you responsible for Charlie in the UK?** (Do you and Charlie live in the UK?)

2. **Do you have any immigration restrictions** that prevent you from claiming public funds, or are you settled in the UK with the right to claim benefits?

Once I have these final details, I'll be able to give you a full assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility for all three children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in Manchester and Charlie lives here with me. And no, I don't have any immigration restrictions - I'm settled here with the right to claim benefits.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Complete

Thank you for providing all the information. I've now assessed your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children. Here's the outcome:

---

### **Alex (20 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE ❌**

Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex for two reasons:
- **Age**: Alex is 20 and not in approved education or training. Child Benefit stops at 16, or can continue up to 20 only if the young person is in approved full-time non-advanced education or training.
- **Hospital stay**: Alex has been in full-time NHS hospital care for 13 weeks (over 12 weeks). Under the rules, you cannot receive Child Benefit for a child in hospital or care paid for by the NHS for more than 12 weeks, even if you contribute to their costs by buying them things.

---

### **Blake (16 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE ❌**

Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake doesn't live with you (they live with their other parent)
- You don't contribute to Blake's upkeep financially
- Blake has left school and is working full-time, not enrolled in any approved training program

When a child doesn't live with you, you can only claim Child Benefit if you contribute to their upkeep at a rate of at least the Child Benefit amount. Since you're not able to contribute financially at the moment, you cannot claim for Blake.

---

### **Charlie (5 years old) - ELIGIBLE ✅**

**Good news!** You ARE eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie because:
- Charlie is 5 years old (under 16)
- Charlie lives with you in the UK
- You are responsible for Charlie's day-to-day care and needs
- You're settled in the UK with the right to claim benefits
- There are no special circumstances affecting eligibility

---

### **Summary**

You can claim Child Benefit for **Charlie only**. You should apply for Child Benefit for Charlie if you haven't already done so.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your specific situation?

reasonings: Alex is 20 years old and not in approved education or training, which means they are over the age limit for Child Benefit (which stops at 16, or up to 20 if in approved education/training). Additionally, Alex has been in full-time NHS hospital care for 13 weeks (over 12 weeks), and under the rules, you cannot get Child Benefit for a child in hospital or care paid for by the NHS for more than 12 weeks, even if you contribute to their costs. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 16 years old and has left school. Although Blake is registered with the careers service, they are working full-time and not enrolled in any approved education or training program. Blake also does not live with you (they live with their other parent), and you do not contribute to Blake's upkeep financially. Under Child Benefit rules, when a child doesn't live with you, you can only claim if you contribute to their upkeep at a rate of at least the Child Benefit amount. Since you don't contribute anything, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 5 years old, lives with you in the UK, you are responsible for their day-to-day care and needs, and there are no special circumstances affecting their eligibility. You are settled in the UK with the right to claim benefits. Charlie meets all the eligibility criteria: they are under 16, live with you, and you are responsible for them. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have three children with different circumstances. For Alex (20), eligibility is affected by both their age (over the limit as they're not in approved education) and their extended NHS hospital stay of over 12 weeks. For Blake (16), although they're within the age range, they don't live with you and you don't contribute to their upkeep, which means you cannot claim. For Charlie (5), all eligibility criteria are met as they live with you, you're responsible for them, and they're under 16. Overall, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Charlie only.
